Hi, and welcome back to Line Sheet. So which one of you hacked Dior=E2=80=
=99s
Instagram account? Or should we start calling it =E2=80=9Cthe exclusive lux=
ury crypto on Pumpfun=E2=80=9D? (I=E2=80=99m so glad I=E2=80=99d never hear=
d of Pump.fun before last night.) The situation was sorted within hours, so=
 nothing to worry about, really.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

Unfortunately, Meghan Markle=E2=80=99s rebranding of American Riviera Orcha=
rd to As Ever was not a hack. As Ever may already be familiar to you as the
Brooklyn-based workwear brand worn by the likes of Jenna Lyons (her famous =
pink boilersuit) and Samira Nasr. (There was even an As Ever x Madewell col=
laboration a few years back.) Anyway, the question of who is advising Markl=
e on such terrible decisions looms large. My best guess: no one.

=C2=A0

Of course, I never question the brilliance of Rachel
Strugatz. Today, she=E2=80=99s back with intel on one of the most important=
 beauty brands to launch since the pandemic: Violette_FR, which illuminates=
 how makeup-artist-led lines are more powerful than ever, at least when exe=
cuted properly. Up top, I=E2=80=99ve got the latest on the Versace saga, ne=
ws about another Neiman Marcus tragedy, and Rachel also has the scoop on th=

* Rachel on another Pat McGrath shake-up: Now that we=E2=80=99ve moved on f=
rom Glass Skin masks (I=E2=80=99m told the number of =E2=80=9Csold out=E2=
=80=9D units totaled 100, by the way, and no, I=E2=80=99m not missing any z=
eros), Pat McGrath Labs is about to endure yet another executive shuffle. I=
=E2=80=99ve learned that C.E.O. Rabih Hamdan resigned after a nine-month te=
nure defined by several rounds of layoffs, rumors of missed deadlines and v=
endor payments, and all-around
tumult. Hamdan was brought in last April to stabilize the business, and a n=
ew executive team was named in July, including a C.M.O. and C.F.O., both of=
 whom are still there, to my knowledge.

On Tuesday, Hamdan sent a pretty unhinged and cryptic farewell email to sta=
ff that was forwarded to me. =E2=80=9CThe business is underpinned by an adm=
irable, iconic, and legendary makeup artist reputation, but I do not think =
that this gives license to make up things,=E2=80=9D read part of the email.=
 After thanking
said legendary makeup artist, Pat McGrath, for convincing him to join the c=
ompany and acknowledging that nothing happens without the founder=E2=80=99s=
 approval, Hamdan wrote that he =E2=80=9Cno longer feels properly equipped =
to continue this journey and to credibly condone its practices.=E2=80=9D I =
heard an email of similar tenor and sentiment was sent to Sephora merchants=
 as well.=C2=A0

It gets messier. A source close to the brand insisted that Hamdan was a =E2=
=80=9Cconsultant=E2=80=9D and
=E2=80=9Cnever the C.E.O.=E2=80=9D=E2=80=93=E2=80=93even though Hamdan=E2=
=80=99s LinkedIn says he=E2=80=99s been C.E.O. of the company since last Ap=
ril. (It=E2=80=99s not unlikely that Hamdan was acting as a fractional C.E.=
O.) =E2=80=9CPat=E2=80=99s title has always been founder, chief creative of=
ficer, and C.E.O.,=E2=80=9D this source said. Interestingly, before joining=
 Pat McGrath Labs, Hamdan was chief of strategy at Kiko Milano, the =E2=80=
=9CItalian Mac=E2=80=9D that, as I reported, just poached Drew Elliott, MAC=
 Cosmetics=E2=80=99 global creative director.
=E2=80=94Rachel Strugatz
* We just really want to know what=E2=80=99s going to happen with Versace: =
Today in New York, Capri C.E.O. and chairman John Idol hosted the company=
=E2=80=99s investor day, about two weeks after its 2024 numbers were releas=
ed. The expectation, of course, was that Idol would share details about the=
 future of the company and its brands=E2=80=94particularly Versace, which i=
s tiny compared to Michael Kors (a projected $810 million
in revenue in 2025 versus $3 billion) but also very much for sale, accordin=
g to everyone but Idol, who acted business-as-usual during the presentation=
 and deployed every piece of M.B.A. jargon under the sun. (There was much t=
alk of =E2=80=9Cstorytelling=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Comnichannel optimization=
=2E=E2=80=9D)=C2=A0

The big takeaway is that the group, which owns Kors, Versace, and Jimmy Cho=
o, is going to shrink to grow=E2=80=94that dreaded term=E2=80=94with a cons=
ervative return-to-growth plan targeted for 2027 that will
include the closure of about 175 stores over the next two years. The compan=
y is also planning on increasing prices at its outlet stores. The market re=
acted favorably to that news, with shares up a couple percentage points in =
early trading.=C2=A0

Versace was the first of the three brands to present its plan, which includ=
es broadening its price architecture to appeal to the high high end and the=
 not-so-high end. C.E.O. Emmanuel Gintzburger admitted that
the brand may have tried to go too upmarket too fast, and presented a few r=
emedies for current operational problems. These will include layoffs, which=
 started last week, with approximately 29 positions being eliminated, I=E2=
=80=99m told. (A rep for Versace declined to comment.)=C2=A0

This culling, across departments, is both expected and necessary. But does =
any of this matter if the company is about to be acquired by the Prada Grou=
p? Just this morning, Bloomberg
reported ( https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-02-19/prada-works-w=
ith-advisers-to-evaluate-versace-for-possible-bid?utm_campaign=3DLine+Sheet=
+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F19%2F25%29&utm_content=3DLine+Sheet+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%28=

=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ) that the Italian company, which owns Prada, Miu Miu=
, and Church=E2=80=99s, has =E2=80=9Ckicked off a full review=E2=80=9D of V=
ersace=E2=80=99s financials. In an ideal scenario for most people involved,=
 the Prada Group would acquire the business and former Miu Miu design direc=
tor Dario Vitale would become a sort of companion to
matriarch Donatella=E2=80=94who, by the way, was not mentioned in Gintzburg=
er=E2=80=99s presentation. If Prada Group were to acquire Versace, many of =
these plans would likely be unwound, from the marketing strategy to the pro=
duct pricing strategy. Idol didn=E2=80=99t say anything about the sale spec=
ulation during investor day. But no analyst asked=E2=80=A6

Perhaps there is a world in which Idol decided to hold on to Jimmy Choo and=

or Tapestry announced this morning that it was selling the shoe brand Stuar=
t Weitzman (a sort of mid-market Jimmy Choo) to the owners of Famous Footwe=
ar for $105 million. Just a decade ago, the business was acquired for $574 =
million.

inue to take
center stage=E2=80=94and an increasing amount of precious shelf space at Se=
phora. But as French makeup line Violette_FR hopes to prove, it=E2=80=99s a=
s much about the product as the artistry.

In recent years, awards season has become the Super Bowl for makeup artists=
=E2=80=94an annual
opportunity to paint and contour as many famous faces as possible on red ca=
rpets. It=E2=80=99s also free marketing for makeup artists, themselves. Whi=
le celebrities rarely succeed with their own beauty ventures (J.Lo, Jared L=
eto, Brad Pitt, Gwen Stefani=E2=80=A6 I could go on), several top makeup ar=
tists (Mario Dedivanovic, Patrick Ta, Isamaya Ffrench, and Bobbi Brown, amo=
ng
them) have been able to capitalize on their A-list client relationships and=
 media exposure to build formidable brands.

=C2=A0

The flurry of red carpet activity has been driven, in part, by growing comp=
etition. There are now dozens of artist-led ventures spanning every style o=
f makeup, at every size and stage of their lifecycles, chasing the spoils o=
f this multibillion-dollar industry. Dedivanovic=E2=80=99s
Makeup by Mario, which he launched just five years ago, is now estimated to=
 be doing about $200 million in sales. Charlotte Tilbury, which was launche=
d in 2013, was acquired by Puig at a $1.2 billion valuation.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

In fact, makeup now accounts for the largest share of revenue at Sephora, t=
he top prestige beauty retailer, which is especially bullish on the subcate=
gory of artist-led brands. In the
last year, Patrick Ta increased its space in-store, and the same goes for G=
ucci Westman=E2=80=99s Westman Atelier, one of Lauren and my all-time favor=
ite makeup brands, which now has distribution in all doors. This week, Seph=
ora is launching the 4-year-old brand of former Est=C3=A9e Lauder global be=
auty director and Guerlain creative director Violette Serrat, Violette_FR, =
and the company also has plans to stock Fara Homidi=E2=80=99s namesake line
in-store later this year. Earlier this month, a decidedly more sanitized ve=
rsion of Isamaya Ffrench=E2=80=99s brand hit the retailer, too.

Alas, there=E2=80=99s only so much shelf space physically available in Seph=
ora: For every up-and-coming brand the retailer launches, another must be d=
epreciated or deprioritized. I hear the retailer has already downsized Pat =
McGrath Labs, with further reductions to come, and that Bobbi Brown, which
basically created the artist-led category, is seeing cuts all around. I=E2=
=80=99m also told that Charlotte Tilbury, despite being a top 10 brand, wil=
l get less real estate due to its recent expansion at Ulta. (Brands lose va=
lue for Sephora when they forgo exclusivity.)

=C2=A0

Sure, there are some exceptions to this Darwinian war against all: Fran=C3=
=A7ois Nars=E2=80=99s Nars is still a Sephora bestseller 31 years
after its debut. But for almost everyone else, it=E2=80=99s a constant batt=
le for consumer attention that has only gotten more difficult as newer entr=
ants leverage both retail and D.T.C. channels. Meanwhile, Sephora itself re=
mains the rare brick-and-mortar distributor that has the ability to make, o=
r break, celebrity- and artist-led brands.

The
Artist=E2=80=99s Way
--------------

Violette_FR, in particular, is set to launch in 65 stores in the coming day=
s, with a
planned rollout of close to 250 doors by year=E2=80=99s end. Selfishly, I=
=E2=80=99m excited about this one. Serrat is a true makeup artist, who is a=
s comfortable on YouTube, where she has more than 300,000 subscribers for h=
er makeup tutorials, as in the boardroom. The brand, itself, is beautiful, =
and the products are a physical manifestation of the founder, who actually =
is French and not just cosplaying the =E2=80=9CFrench girl beauty=E2=80=9D =
thing. It=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9Cno makeup makeup=E2=80=9D for the person who s=
till wears a
semi-sheer, matte red lip or blue eyeliner.

=C2=A0

One makeup executive told me that Serrat makes her expertise accessible to =
regular people=E2=80=94unlike, say, Pat McGrath, an immensely talented arti=
st whose inability to lean into the mainstream has limited her commercial s=
uccess. Both are masters of their craft, but Serrat is much better at trans=
lating her aesthetic for laypeople. Serrat=E2=80=99s ethos isn=E2=80=99t to
teach you how to paint a feathered, French girl lip; it=E2=80=99s to produc=
e a new lipstick product that produces the same effect. The result is brill=
iant.

=C2=A0

I=E2=80=99m told Sephora is really getting behind Violette_FR and will sell=
 all four of the brand=E2=80=99s product categories: primarily makeup, but =
also skincare, fragrance, and hair. =E2=80=9CNot many brands have permissio=
n to extend to all four
categories so early in their lifecycle,=E2=80=9D said a person close to the=
 brand. =E2=80=9CViolette was able to prove out that she=E2=80=99s not just=
 dabbling in things like skincare=E2=80=94Boum-Boum Milk is a hero product.=
=E2=80=9D=C2=A0

=C2=A0

The brand=E2=80=99s success also owes much to its owner=E2=80=99s patience.=
 Serrat once told me that she didn=E2=80=99t run any paid marketing campaig=
ns for almost her first full year in business because she wanted to see
how consumers reacted to the products without pulling any customer acquisit=
ion levers=E2=80=94an unprecedented strategy. And while similar brands may =
have entered Sephora or Ulta within a year of launching, according to the p=
erson close to the brand, Serrat =E2=80=9Cchose to test out authentic Frenc=
h positioning and distribution=E2=80=9D at places like Le Bon Marche and Oh=
 My Cream!=E2=80=94a French beauty retailer with about 30 doors=E2=80=94bef=
ore entering into a major retail partnership.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

Now, after about four years, it feels like Violette_FR is on the verge of t=
aking off. In December, Serrat raised a Series B led by Silas Capital=E2=80=
=94also a backer of Makeup by Mario=E2=80=94and brought on Est=C3=A9e Laude=
r veteran Heidi Merris as president. (Monogram Capital Partners, Female Fou=
nders Fund, Greycroft, and Felix Capital are also investors.) I know a liqu=
idity event is almost always the ultimate goal, but Violette_FR doesn=E2=80=
=99t
feel like it was created for the purpose of scaling and flipping it to a st=
rategic=E2=80=94a rarity in this industry, or any industry, really.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

But whatever success Violette_FR finds is at least partly due to beauty con=
sumers with less tolerance for gimmicks. =E2=80=9CThe consumer is getting s=
marter about what feels like a B.S. brand,=E2=80=9D one investor told me, =
=E2=80=9Cand they=E2=80=99re moving toward brands that have a founder with =
a real
reason to speak. That=E2=80=99s what the heart of what artistry brands are.=
=E2=80=9D

;font-size:17px;font-weight:400;letter-spacing:0px;line-height:150%;text-al=
ign:left;mso-line-height-alt:25.5px;"><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0p=
x;">In recent years, awards season has become the Super Bowl for makeup art=
ists=E2=80=94an annual
opportunity to paint and contour as many famous faces as possible on red ca=
rpets. It=E2=80=99s also free marketing for makeup artists, themselves. Whi=
le celebrities rarely succeed with their own beauty ventures (<strong>J.Lo<=
/strong>, <strong>Jared Leto</strong>, <strong>Brad Pitt</strong>, <strong>=
Gwen Stefani</strong>=E2=80=A6 I could go on), several top makeup artists (=
<strong>Mario Dedivanovic</strong>, <strong>Patrick Ta</strong>, <strong>Is=
amaya Ffrench</strong>, and <strong>Bobbi Brown</strong>, among
them) have been able to capitalize on their A-list client relationships and=
 media exposure to build formidable brands.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-=
bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">The flurry =
of red carpet activity has been driven, in part, by growing competition. Th=
ere are now <em>dozens</em> of artist-led ventures spanning every style of =
makeup, at every size and stage of their lifecycles, chasing the spoils of =
this multibillion-dollar industry. Dedivanovic=E2=80=99s
Makeup by Mario, which he launched just five years ago, is now estimated to=
 be doing about $200 million in sales. Charlotte Tilbury, which was launche=
d in 2013, was acquired by Puig at a $1.2 <em>billion</em> valuation.=C2=A0=
</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0=
;margin-bottom:0px;">In fact, makeup now accounts for the largest share of =
revenue at Sephora, the top prestige beauty retailer, which is especially b=
ullish on the subcategory of artist-led brands. In the
last year, Patrick Ta increased its space in-store, and the same goes for <=
strong>Gucci Westman</strong>=E2=80=99s Westman Atelier, one of <strong>Lau=
ren</strong> and my all-time favorite makeup brands, which now has distribu=
tion in all doors. This week, Sephora is launching the 4-year-old brand of =
former Est=C3=A9e Lauder global beauty director and Guerlain creative direc=
tor <strong>Violette Serrat</strong>, Violette_FR, and the company also has=
 plans to stock <strong>Fara Homidi</strong>=E2=80=99s namesake line
in-store later this year. Earlier this month, a decidedly more sanitized ve=
rsion of <strong>Isamaya Ffrench</strong>=E2=80=99s brand hit the retailer,=
 too.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;"><br/>Alas, there=E2=80=99=
s only so much shelf space <em>physically available</em> in Sephora: For ev=
ery up-and-coming brand the retailer launches, another must be depreciated =
or deprioritized. I hear the retailer has already downsized Pat McGrath Lab=
s, with further reductions to come, and that Bobbi Brown, which
basically created the artist-led category, is seeing cuts all around. I=E2=
=80=99m also told that Charlotte Tilbury, despite being a top 10 brand, wil=
l get less real estate due to its recent expansion at Ulta. (Brands lose va=
lue for Sephora when they forgo exclusivity.)</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margi=
n-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;">Sure, there are some except=
ions to this Darwinian war against all: <strong>Fran=C3=A7ois Nars</strong>=
=E2=80=99s Nars is still a Sephora bestseller <em>31</em> <em>years</em>
after its debut. But for almost everyone else, it=E2=80=99s a constant batt=
le for consumer attention that has only gotten more difficult as newer entr=
ants leverage both retail and D.T.C. channels. Meanwhile, Sephora itself re=
mains the rare brick-and-mortar distributor that has the ability to make, o=
r break, celebrity- and artist-led brands.</p></div>
</td></tr></tbody></table>

Artist=E2=80=99s Way</strong></h3>

<table width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellpadding=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" role=

reak:break-word;"><tbody><tr><td style=3D"padding-bottom:32px;"><div style=
=3D"color:#000000;direction:ltr;font-family:'Inter','Arial'=
;font-size:17px;font-weight:400;letter-spacing:0px;line-height:150%;text-al=
ign:left;mso-line-height-alt:25.5px;"><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0p=
x;">Violette_FR, in particular, is set to launch in 65 stores in the coming=
 days, with a
planned rollout of close to 250 doors by year=E2=80=99s end. Selfishly, I=
=E2=80=99m excited about this one. Serrat is a true makeup <em>artist</em>,=
 who is as comfortable on YouTube, where she has more than 300,000 subscrib=
ers for her makeup tutorials, as in the boardroom. The brand, itself, is be=
autiful, and the products are a physical manifestation of the founder, who =
<em>actually</em> is French and not just cosplaying the =E2=80=9CFrench gir=
l beauty=E2=80=9D thing. It=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9Cno makeup makeup=E2=80=9D fo=
r the person who still wears a
semi-sheer, matte red lip or blue eyeliner.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-=
bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">One makeup =
executive told me that Serrat makes her expertise accessible to <em>regular=
 </em>people=E2=80=94unlike, say, Pat McGrath, an immensely talented artist=
 whose inability to lean into the mainstream has limited her commercial suc=
cess. Both are masters of their craft, but Serrat is much better at transla=
ting her aesthetic for laypeople. Serrat=E2=80=99s ethos isn=E2=80=99t to
teach you how to paint a feathered, French girl lip; it=E2=80=99s to produc=
e a new lipstick <em>product</em> that produces the same effect. The result=
 is brilliant.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p sty=
le=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">I=E2=80=99m told Sephora is <em>really <=
/em>getting behind Violette_FR and will sell all <em>four</em> of the brand=
=E2=80=99s product categories: primarily makeup, but also skincare, fragran=
ce, and hair. =E2=80=9CNot many brands have permission to extend to all fou=
r
categories so early in their lifecycle,=E2=80=9D said a person close to the=
 brand. =E2=80=9CViolette was able to prove out that she=E2=80=99s not just=
 dabbling in things like skincare=E2=80=94Boum-Boum Milk is a hero product.=
=E2=80=9D=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p st=
yle=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">The brand=E2=80=99s success also owes m=
uch to its owner=E2=80=99s patience. Serrat once told me that she didn=E2=
=80=99t run <em>any</em> paid marketing campaigns for almost her first full=
 year in business because she wanted to see
how consumers reacted to the products without pulling any customer acquisit=
ion levers=E2=80=94an unprecedented strategy. And while similar brands may =
have entered Sephora or Ulta within a year of launching, according to the p=
erson close to the brand, Serrat =E2=80=9Cchose to test out authentic Frenc=
h positioning and distribution=E2=80=9D at places like Le Bon Marche and Oh=
 My Cream!=E2=80=94a French beauty retailer with about 30 doors=E2=80=94bef=
ore entering into a major retail partnership.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0=
;margin-bottom:
0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">Now, after about f=
our years, it feels like Violette_FR is on the verge of taking off. In Dece=
mber, Serrat raised a Series B led by Silas Capital=E2=80=94also a backer o=
f Makeup by Mario=E2=80=94and brought on Est=C3=A9e Lauder veteran <strong>=
Heidi Merris</strong> as president. (Monogram Capital Partners, Female Foun=
ders Fund, Greycroft, and Felix Capital are also investors.) I know a liqui=
dity event is almost always the ultimate goal, but Violette_FR doesn=E2=80=
=99t
<em>feel </em>like it was created for the purpose of scaling and flipping i=
t to a strategic=E2=80=94a rarity in this industry, or any industry, really=
=2E=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margi=
n-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"m=
argin:0;">But whatever success Violette_FR finds is at least partly due to =
beauty consumers with less tolerance for gimmicks. =E2=80=9CThe consumer is=
 getting smarter about what feels like a B.S. brand,=E2=80=9D one investor =
told me, =E2=80=9Cand they=E2=80=99re moving toward brands that have a foun=
der with a real
reason to speak. That=E2=80=99s what the heart of what artistry brands are.=
=E2=80=9D</p></div>
